At the current LAES stock price, investors are paying $2.95 per share, but about $2.35 sits as cash on the balance sheet. Which means the market is pricing the operating business, including the semiconductor IP, post-quantum security stack, and IC'Alps integration, at around $0.60 per share.

At a current market cap of around ~$657 million, LAES may appear expensive on the headline for a company in early commercialization, but comparing that against the peers most investors in this space are watching, I find a clear valuation disconnect.

SEALSQ's high cash reserve coupled with zero debt on the balance sheet means EV compresses to ~$133 million. Putting that against FY25 sales of $18.3 million implies that LAES is trading around ~7.2x FY25 sales. Management is guiding for sales growth between 50% and 100% of that, and the preliminary Q1 FY26 update already shows SEALSQ is off to a solid start in FY26, as revenue in Q1 is expected to exceed $4 million when the audited results are released. That sales figure will be 100% higher than Q1 FY25 sales. At the midpoint of management's FY26 sales guidance, LAES is effectively trading at 4.8x forward EV/Sales. SEALSQ's high cash balance and zero debt compress EV and drive this discount relative to peers.

From the preceding table, where I have manually sourced and calculated the respective values for Cash per Share and EV/Sales, as financial websites present varying and outdated shares outstanding data for some of these companies, I also find LAES to be the most discounted at the current share price in terms of Cash per Share. Peers have between ~8% and ~20% of the current value backed by the balance sheet, while LAES has ~80% of the current share price backed by cash.

On the Q1 FY26 preliminary update, management has not deviated from its FY25-end guidance. I looked for any shift in tone that could imply an early downward revision of FY26 guidance and found none throughout the update. Management reiterated the $200 million active pipeline of potential revenue opportunities between 2026 and 2029.

Risks and Takeaway

While I've highlighted SEALSQ's strong liquidity position as the foundation of the current thesis, a continuity of this per-share value expansion means future dilution must remain accretive, which itself hinges on market pricing and investor demand for LAES. In the event that weak sentiment or a sustained decline in share price leads to management raising capital at a price below cash per share, that would spell immediate value destruction and erosion of cash per share for existing holders. That said, with over $525 million and a current annual burn of less than 10% of that amount, this risk of forced equity raises during periods of weak equity pricing is low in the near term as the runway remains comfortably extended for SEALSQ.

Another risk lies more fundamentally in the fact that revenue is still early and relatively small compared to pipeline. Revenue for FY25 was $18.3 million, with much of the forward expectation still anchored on the $200 million commercial pipeline management has outlined. In the event that revenue conversion from this pipeline stalls or declines drastically while burn increases, the attractive EV/Sales that SEALSQ currently has will become harder to justify.

At current levels, the balance sheet is the foundation of LAES thesis. It works while cash per share rises faster than dilution. This is the key indicator to track. If cash per share stalls or drops while share count keeps expanding, that edge disappears with it.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SM Energy: Strong Buying Trends Since February Merger Should Continue
data.description:
In late January, SM Energy Company (SM) completed its merger with Civitas Resources, another U.S.-focused oil/gas producer. The promoted payoff will be a more diversified operator, with a goal of cost synergies and reduced debt (close to $8 billion in combined debt existed at the end of 2025). The new company has already sold off some South Texas assets for $950 million in cash and refinanced $1 billion in debt at lower interest rates because of Wall Street's positive rerating of long-term business risks.

With a more limited hedge book than other U.S. producers, the Iran war spike in global prices for energy should flow rather directly to shareholder value. The push to lower interest expense/debt and the ability to cut operating costs should also benefit the stock quote throughout 2026.

Seeking Alpha analyst Ronald Ferrie did a wonderful job weeks ago here, outlining management's targeting of debt reduction during 2026. Below are summaries of the new SM Energy setup and goals for operations, taken from the late February release of Q4 2025 earnings.

In the end, the pull for my investment capital has been a personal belief since the end of 2025 that sharply higher oil/gas prices were destined for 2026. After 3+ years of downcycle in the energy sector as a whole, we were getting overdue for an upcycle, especially in crude oil. In December here, I explained the bullish logic for crude oil to follow gold/silver higher after last year's massive rally in precious metals. I was one of a scant few on Seeking Alpha calling for oil prices above $100 this year as a real possibility before any hint of bombs falling on Iran entered the equation.

Today, I am even more bullish on crude oil and natural gas pricing from the surprise Iran war development. My view is crude will average over US$100 a barrel for the rest of 2026 (from lingering production headaches and hard-to-shake supply shortages from lost Middle East sources), which remains far above Wall Street forecasts of a dip back toward $75-80 by the end of the year (using CME futures pricing). For sure, even $75 a barrel would be good news for SM's quote, but $100+ would be dramatically better. EPS unhedged could be running at an annualized run rate above $9, versus current Wall Street estimates around $7.

If my bullish outlook for both crude oil and SM Energy operations proves correct, considerable upside in price remains. This view also seems to match the technical trading evidence conclusion, reinforcing my optimism.

Let's dig a little deeper to understand why a $50-60 SM stock price target is not out of the question over the next 12-18 months.

Deep Value Candidate

Honestly, for a mid-major to leading U.S. oil/gas producer, SM Energy may offer the cheapest valuation available to investors wanting exposure to the petroleum sector or a speculation on crude oil prices remaining high this year.

The recently combined company is projected to be priced at just 3.9x earnings for 2027 (using conservative commodity price estimates). This valuation stands out as a unique buy for investors, assuming oil/gas prices remain elevated into next year. The fossil fuel E&P group median average drawn below is 10x.

Even after we include SM's extra debt load vs. peers, the total enterprise valuation on core cash EBITDA is equally compelling, sitting at just 1.95x 2026 estimates. The peer median average is 4.7x.

Wall Street's rationale for a lower valuation has to do with excessive debt. Yes, I get it. Below is a graph of financial debt to equity of 1.3x for pre-merger SM only. I am estimating the 2026 ending number, including Civitas debt, alongside up to $2 billion paid off during the next 9-12 months, will bring a similar to marginally lower ratio. The peer group median average is around 0.35x.

The good news is yet higher oil/gas commodity pricing should allow the company to rapidly pay down debt with existing cash flow. If you give management a few years of smart decision-making, I fully expect the debt-to-equity ratio to fall well under 1.0x. Of course, the lower this number goes, the greater the equity valuation should become.

Seeking Alpha's overall Quant Valuation Grade of "A-" highlights the unusual upside potential for SM shareholders on any rerating of the business toward industry norms. If you will allow me to summarize the SM investment setup, I believe it could be one of the top risk-adjusted "call-option" like opportunities on rising crude oil and natural gas quotes available to retail investors currently.

Natural Gas Kicker

Another bullish point to make is that a little over half of SM production is related to crude oil, while the other half relies on natural gas. With a considerable amount of natural gas exports to the world shut-in presently in the Middle East, natural gas prices may also begin to trend higher for the remainder of 2026. Prices in the rest of the world were already substantially above U.S. quotes before President Trump's attack, while Texas (the location of a majority of SM assets) is oversupplied with even lower pricing than New York markets. This leaves all kinds of upside revenue and profit potential on the table if exploding AI data center demand combines with lower production rates from the Middle East to boost gas and liquid natural gas pricing.

My view is we could be on the verge of a historic advance in natural gas into 2027 (especially if we get a bad hurricane season that hits offshore production in America around Louisiana and Texas). I would peg sub-$3.00 MMBtu natural gas as one of the most undervalued and underappreciated commodities in the world, ready for a monster rally.

When we compare natural gas to crude oil quotes (unusually weak prices at $2.71 vs. $99 per barrel) or precious metals like silver (all-time lows in comparison at $2.71 to $75 oz.), it's not hard to see a mismatch has developed. Given natural gas prices jump back above $5 this year, perhaps headed toward $10 next year as our future, SM Energy will morph into a very desirable asset for energy and value investors alike (regardless of oversized gains in crude oil).

Strong Buying Momentum

I started purchasing SM Energy shares in December-January, with the merger news coming right at a bottom in oil/gas quotes (per my research and conclusions). Since the middle of February, SM's stock quote has taken off. The Iran war added buying fuel to the fire. And I am projecting materially higher crude oil and natural gas pricing in America will propel/support the share price for many months (if not years) to come.

You can see the clear reversal from selling to buying momentum in SM on the 18-month chart below. I have boxed in green super-strong relative strength vs. the S&P 500 index since February, alongside steady progress in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, Negative Volume Index, and On Balance Volume. As of today, price has recaptured its 14-day, and 50-day moving averages (after a 4-week selloff), with the 200-day MA now turning higher. We could be on the cusp of another leg of price gains to a level above $35 in May-June.

While heavy volume buying is apparent on the daily chart during early 2026, price gains have actually been occurring at a below-normal volume rate when you look at a Renko chart. A standard-setting 1-year Renko is drawn below, highlighting the ordinary to lower-than-typical volume bars necessary to move price a set amount (boxed in gold). The heightened volume of merger-related selling/arbitrage in SM from November to December has disappeared with the Iran war outbreak.

Investment Risks

What could derail my optimism about SM Energy? There are three main risks I worry about. The obvious operating issue management has control over is the debt situation. At present, SM does carry too much debt. However, high oil/gas prices should (will) open up the rare opportunity to deleverage quickly. I suggest management take the gift as a way to rerate the common equity higher on Wall Street.

Two other risks outside of company control are changing energy prices and stock market values. If a serious recession with demand destruction globally happens in the second half of 2026, fossil fuel commodity prices could collapse or just fail to rise much above current levels. This outcome would almost surely bring an estimated trading range for the stock of $25-35 over the next 12-18 months.

The final risk involves a Wall Street stock market crash scenario. If today's near-record overvaluation (using price-to-sales, CAPE Ratio analysis, and total market capitalization to GDP) is decompressed rapidly in a recession soon, U.S. equities could easily drop -25% to -50% over a period of months, not years. If this is the future, SM Energy will find it difficult to climb in value, no matter how oil/gas prices fluctuate.

Upside Forecast

Oil/gas upcycles generally last years, not months. Most analysts on Wall Street are hoping for a resolution and peace deal in the Iran war to bring back supply stability and lower prices this year. I doubt that is what lies ahead for the petroleum market supply/demand balance.

Having better odds is the "oversupply" of oil and gas, following the price spike in both commodities during 2022's Russian invasion of Ukraine, has ended. We have entered a several-year period of supply shortages, in one form/degree or another. The typical upcycle does not end until new supplies of oil/gas are developed to rebalance the marketplace. Already, I am reading about estimates of 6-12 months to get Middle East supplies up and running at previous levels, rebuilt after their destruction in March-April. Plus, drilling is only starting to ramp up in America and elsewhere to capture prices above $100 a barrel. It will take time for these new supplies to get to market.

For natural gas, the primary demand catalyst for a bull move revolves around Artificial Intelligence adoption and usage. AI power requirements are enormous, where electricity demand spiking and new natural gas plants being built to meet data center growth. It could be that this technological invention for mankind becomes a permanent source of outsized electricity demand. Such will have to be met with record-high natural gas production into 2027-30. (I do also like/own renewable energy sources like solar assets to meet rising electricity demand.)

So, material supply shortages for natural gas could be on deck for the global economy (AI data centers are under construction all over the planet), compounded by Middle East volumes moving into reverse.

Using $100+ crude oil and $5 natural gas estimates for 2027, SM Energy could be delivering EPS above $10, meaning today's P/E is under 3x forward results! Given a reduced-debt balance sheet, a P/E above 5x seems logical (still under the industry average), good for a near-future stock quote of $50. And, if energy shortages appear to be a longer-term problem, Wall Street could easily put a 6x or 7x multiple on SM's after-tax income generation. That gives us a best-case upside target of $60-70 per share over 12-18 months.

Anyway, that's the bullish SM Energy argument as I see it. I rate SM a Buy under $35 for a 12-month outlook. I am projecting the stock will continue to widely outperform the S&P 500, similar to early 2026's experience.

Because of its ultra-low valuation and ramping cash flow/earnings, alongside the favorable U.S. location of its assets, SM Energy is now a prime takeover candidate for larger oil/gas outfits (in my view). I would not be surprised by an offer/bid for the stock before the end of summer.

data.title: Stack Capital Group: Easy Way To Invest In SpaceX
data.description:
Summary of the Company:

Stack Capital Group (STCK:CA) is a holding company that invests in private companies. Its business objective is to invest in equity, debt, and/or other securities of growth-to-late-stage private businesses.

I want to mention this stock because it's listed on a Canadian stock exchange and may have been overlooked by many American investors. Stack gives regular investors the ability to participate in private investments. Currently one of the largest private investments they have on their books is SpaceX. Which also happens to be one of the most anticipated IPOs of the year.

They primarily hold private companies, but they also reserve the right to keep any shares of companies that have gone public; this can be seen from their current holding of CoreWeave (CRWV). This is beneficial since it increases liquidity for this holding company.

Why Consider Private Companies:

The private markets have been growing fiercely over the last few decades. You can see it from the infographic below. This growth means there are more opportunities in the private sector than in the very "market efficient" public sector. There are many reasonable explanations as to why there are many more private companies today than public ones:

  • Longer-term focus (not chasing quarterly earnings reports)
  • Easier access to private funding
  • Compliance costs and the regulatory burden of public company disclosures keep private companies private.
  • Dilution of founders’ control.

Whatever the reasons, the result is clear: many large-scale companies prefer to stay private rather than go public. The numbers are striking; almost 3 times more investable companies are private now than public.

They do generate income from various sources, such as income from interest sitting in deposit accounts, unrealized gains/losses of investments, unrealized gains/losses from FX changes, and sales of private investments to other private investors.

Because a large part of their income is derived from the valuation differences in investment holdings, their income will be quite volatile compared to most regular publicly listed companies.

With Stack, now accredited and non-accredited investors can invest in these highly anticipated and desired private companies. Having a public company holding private investments is a great strategy to provide investors with private investments and liquidity, something that is very hard to get when you are a direct holder of private equity.

Stack Capital invests in companies at the later stages of being private; these would be the growth stage and late stage.

Benefits of Investing in Growth and Late-Stage Private Deals:

Growth and late-stage private investing offer exposure to companies that have already proven their business model, have revenue growth, and have a clear path to profitability. This reduces much of the early uncertainty while still providing significant upside potential. In contrast, seed and early-stage venture capital involves investing when products may not be fully developed, revenues are minimal or nonexistent, and success depends heavily on the founders’ ability to execute. Basically, in the seed capital round, you are investing in the founder and idea, while in the later stages, you are investing in an operating business.

Investment Strategy & Focus:

They are looking for businesses that "offer a margin of safety". They are able to achieve this margin by targeting investments into companies that have:

  • Strong Growth Rates
  • Strong Balance Sheets
  • Experienced Management
  • Attractive valuations

These metrics are very reasonable since Stack Capital targets investments in the growth and late stages of the private company life cycle. As is implied in the name, the growth phase of a business needs to see "growth". But if the management team is skilled and able to execute on goals, they should be running a growing business that is nearing profitability, if not already profitable. Also, in general, due to traditional discounts applied to private investments (liquidity discount, marketability discount, information risk discount, etc.), there should be many attractively priced private companies as investment targets.

Last year the CEO was quoted saying, "We’re seeing a robust IPO environment alongside strong momentum in the private markets—particularly among disruptive companies in AI, LLMs, and robotics,"

"These market dynamics continue to create attractive opportunities, and we remain focused on backing category-leading businesses with the potential to deliver meaningful, long-term value for our shareholders."

As I read this, I am left with the impression that more of their investments will come from AI going forward.

Valuation of Common Shares:

From a valuation standpoint, there is a book value provided by Stack on a regular basis. Their investments are revalued at fair value quarterly. Depending on the type of security, they have different ways of finding the fair value of assets. The main valuation techniques they use are recent funding round valuations, deriving value based on comparable public companies, and discounted cash flow or other valuation models.

The Book Value Per Share as listed on their latest quarterly statement is $15.35 on Dec 31, 2025. Which comes very close to the market price on that day of $15.44.

Currently it trades around $26 CAD. This price is after SpaceX had mentioned that it was looking to IPO soon. One of the uncertainties of having private investments valued internally is the dependence on management’s assumptions for growth rates, discount rates, etc. From the published documents, their auditor looks over the assumptions management has made and decides if they are reasonable. Having a 3rd party look over the assumptions made should alleviate any uneasiness some investors might have.

Value Over Time:

Stack Capital Group has done ok since going public in June of 2021. As of this year, STCK is outperforming the Nasdaq. But it has been lagging the Nasdaq since it went public. I attribute this recent ascent to the fortunes of SpaceX, which has gained a lot of attention since announcing their desire to go public this year. Sudden gains and little correlation are to be expected with private companies historically since they are valued infrequently.

Buybacks and Private Placements:

Stack operates in a fast-moving environment. They are still actively raising capital for new investments while at the same time initiating share buybacks. The message I take away from these two actions is there are good investment opportunities out there, and we feel our stock price is undervalued.

April 10, 2026: Stack Capital Group Inc. closed an upsized best efforts concurrent private placement for $31.25 Million; aggregate gross proceeds of $40 million

Dec 1, 2025: Stack Capital will be buying back shares during the 12-month period commencing December 3, 2025, and ending December 2, 2026, of up to 1,095,780 common shares in total, which represents 10% of the “public float” of common shares.

Risks:

There are many risks involved in private markets/investments. Here are 2 points I think are relatively important for this investment.

1. Competition and Technology Risks

Tech trends are changing quickly. With AI having a huge impact across all companies in the economy. In particular, AI is having a big effect on tech companies. There are some companies that are contributing to the growth of AI, and they are being rewarded handsomely. Meanwhile, there are many SaaS companies that are being hit hard by the perception that AI can replace many of the products they offer. That perception is not unwarranted. The average person with little tech background is able to go online and vibe code their way to an application that can solve a problem they have in a relatively short period of time. I have first-hand experience with this, and so does my 4th grader. On the flip side of this, many of these legacy SaaS providers have a deep entrenchment in the infrastructure of these large enterprises. At the end of the day, that deep integration is a fierce barrier for new entrants. It’s a fierce barrier because of simple inertia; it takes a lot of effort to change a business process or a provider that is integrated deeply. Most managers do not want to take the risk of changing a process or providers if it's working fairly well. Simply put, there needs to be a huge advantage for a large enterprise to make huge changes.

2. Reliance on Key Personnel and Risks Associated with the Management Agreement.

This is important for both the key people at Stack Capital Group and the founders running individual private companies. In growing private companies, the CEO is often the single biggest driver of success. They set the vision, attract capital, recruit key talent, and make important decisions. Unlike mature public companies with layers of structure, these companies rely more on the CEO’s ability to plan and execute.

Recommendation:

If you are looking to invest in private companies, this might be the easiest way for retail investors to do so. The structure of this holding company is straightforward, and the investor just needs to decide if they trust the judgement of Stack managers to keep finding new investment targets. This is a great way for investors to indirectly participate in SpaceX (one of the most anticipated IPOs of 2026). Now there are many active fund managers (mutual funds and ETFs) out there that have a small percentage of their holdings invested in SpaceX, but few of them would/could approach almost 30% of their fund's value coming from SpaceX.

I believe this stock is a buy because you get an opportunity to participate in sought-after private companies while having access to liquidity.

At this moment I do not own any shares of STCK, but I am tempted to get some in the near future so I too can tell people I invested in the early days of SpaceX.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fox Corporation: The Market Is Still Missing The Real Value
data.description:
Introduction

This article explains why Fox Corporation (NASDAQ: FOX) is undervalued. The market is still not giving the company full credit for what it owns. Fox is being valued as one broad media company, even though its best assets are stronger than what it looks like at first sight. The company owns a strong cable business in news and sports. It also owns Tubi, which has become a successful digital platform. When these assets are viewed together inside one company, the market appears to apply a discount. When they are viewed differently, the deep value comes out.

Going through a sum-of-the-parts analysis, I get an equity value of about $32.4 billion, or roughly $76.2 per share, giving us a higher double-digit upside relative to the current share price.

The stock does not need an aggressive breakup for the thesis to work. My main case is simpler: if Cable stays strong, Tubi continues to improve, and the market starts valuing the business more clearly, the stock can rerate from here. Ongoing buybacks from normal free cash flow would help, but they are not the base of the thesis.

Company Overview

Fox is a media company with two main reporting segments: Cable Network Programming and Television. Cable Network Programming is the strongest part of the business. It includes Fox News, Fox Sports, FS1, FS2, and Big Ten Network. Television includes the Fox broadcast network, local television stations, and Tubi.

That distinction matters since the market often looks at the company as one broad media group. But the parts are different in quality. Cable is the stronger and more stable part, while Tubi is the main growth asset. The weaker entertainment and content-selling pieces sit alongside them and make the whole company look more mixed, which distorts the overall valuation.

In fiscal 2025, Cable generated $6.93 billion of revenue and $3.03 billion of segment EBITDA. Television generated $9.33 billion of revenue and $945 million of segment EBITDA.

Tubi is now large enough to let it grow on its own. In fiscal 2025, it exceeded $1 billion of revenue for the first time, total view time reached about 11 billion hours, and it reached about 2.2% of all television viewing in the U.S. I believe that is enough scale to treat it as a real digital asset inside the company.

Why The Stock Trades At A Discount

There are a few clear reasons why the stock looks cheap.

First, the market still views Fox mainly as a traditional television company. That keeps the valuation lower than it should be. But Fox is not just a general entertainment television company. Its strongest businesses are live news and live sports. Those remain valuable because they still attract viewers and advertisers and generate fees.

Second, the company’s business mix is uneven. While Cable is high quality, Tubi is a growth asset, and other parts of Television are weaker and more cyclical in nature, adding to volatility in valuation. When these assets are put together in one company, the market often applies a blended discount.

Third, Tubi is still not getting full credit. Tubi has reached a respected scale, but it is still trapped inside the Television segment. As a result, the market does not value it the way it would value a standalone digital platform with similar revenue, users, and growth.

Fourth, the company still carries some complexity through other investments and the FanDuel option. These assets do have value, but they also make the company harder to be valued by investors. FOX carried about $1.249 billion under investments in equity securities, while Flutter disclosed a fair value of about $560 million for the FOX option on FanDuel.

Put simply, the stock does not trade at a discount because the assets are poor. It trades at a discount because the structure is still mixed and the market is not separating the better assets from the weaker ones.

Recent Performance: Positives And Limits

There are real positives in the business too - Cable remains strong as it continues to benefit from affiliate fees, sports, and news. This part of the business is still producing large amounts of cash. Tubi also continues to improve. Revenue is growing, scale is increasing, and profitability appears to be improving. That matters because it gives Fox a more modern digital asset inside the company, which is actively growing.

At the same time, the results are not perfectly clean. The broader Television segment still includes weaker entertainment and content revenue streams. Those pieces can make results look less consistent and can hold back the market’s view of the company.

How The Business Is Being Run

Cable is being run around the assets that still matter most in traditional media: live news and live sports, which I believe is the right approach. These are still the parts of television that attract viewers in real-time and still matter to advertisers and distributors.

Tubi is also being run in a sensible way. Fox is not trying to force it into a cash-heavy subscription model. It is building Tubi as a free ad-supported platform and letting scale improve the economics over time. That fits Fox much better than trying to copy the large subscription streaming platforms.

The weaker issue, however, is that the lower-quality entertainment and content pieces inside Television still make the whole company look more mixed than it really is. So, in my view, management is doing some important things right, but the company still needs to become cleaner and easier for the market to understand.

What To Watch Next

Because the next quarter is close, I do not think the stock case should depend on one quarter. What matters more is what happens over the next few quarters. For Cable, I want to see affiliate and advertising trends stay stable enough to protect the earnings base. For Tubi, I would like to see its scale continue to turn into better monetization. For the rest of Television, I wish to see the weaker parts stop dragging so much on the whole story.

My thesis does not need aggressive growth. It only needs Cable to remain durable, Tubi to keep improving, and the weaker parts of Television to become less important to the valuation.

Competitive Position

Fox is not the same as the weakest legacy media companies. Its strongest assets are live news and live sports, and those remain more valuable than general entertainment because they still bring live audiences and immediate ad demand.

Tubi also sits in a different part of streaming. It is a free ad-supported platform, not a high-spend subscription business. That matters because it gives Fox a digital growth asset without the same level of cash burn that many streaming peers have faced.

Why A Sum-Of-The-Parts View Matters

When I look at Fox, I do not think it makes sense to value the whole company on one single multiple. The better way is to value the main parts separately.

Cable deserves a stronger multiple because it is the best and most stable earnings asset inside the company. The rest of Television deserves a lower multiple because some of those assets are lower quality and more uneven. Tubi deserves its own valuation approach because it is a digital platform with growth, scale, and improving profitability. Then there are the other assets, such as the FanDuel option and other investments, which add further value.

I also do not use SOTP alone. FOX’s cash generation and balance sheet support the idea that the stock is cheap. But the SOTP is still useful because it shows more clearly where the value sits inside the company.

Detailed SOTP Valuation

Let’s go part by part: This SOTP uses a different metric for each part because the businesses are different. Cable is valued on EBITDA, Television ex-Tubi on a lower EBITDA multiple, Tubi on sales, and the financial assets at disclosed marks. The reported company figures come from FOX filings and disclosures. The carve-out items are my own estimates.

Cable Network Programming

I value Cable using an EBITDA multiple because it is the clearest cash-generating asset in the group. Using a trailing EBITDA base of about $3.11 billion and applying a multiple of about 8.75x, Cable appears to be worth roughly $27.2 billion.

That $3.11 billion base is built from FOX’s reported fiscal 2025 Cable segment EBITDA of $3.03 billion and the year-over-year lift in first-half fiscal 2026 Cable segment EBITDA. In simple terms, I start with the reported $3.03 billion and add the roughly $82 million increase between first-half fiscal 2026 Cable EBITDA of $1.487 billion and first-half fiscal 2025 Cable EBITDA of $1.405 billion. That gets me to about $3.11 billion.

Television Excluding Tubi

For the core Television business, excluding Tubi, I use a lower EBITDA multiple because this part of the business is less attractive and more cyclical.

FOX reported fiscal 2025 Television segment EBITDA of $945 million. I separately value Tubi on sales, so I need to remove some Tubi EBITDA from the Television segment to avoid double counting. FOX does not disclose Tubi EBITDA, so I use a simple and conservative bridge. Tubi exceeded $1 billion of revenue in fiscal 2025. I assume only about $95 million of Tubi EBITDA, which implies an EBITDA margin of roughly 8.5% on that revenue base. That leaves about $850 million for legacy Television. This is my estimate, but I believe it is a conservative one rather than an aggressive one.

Tubi

For Tubi, I use a revenue multiple rather than EBITDA because Fox does not disclose enough standalone profit detail for Tubi. Using about $1.1 billion of revenue and a 2.25x EV/Sales multiple, Tubi appears to be worth about $2.5 billion.

Note: I use a 2.25x EV/sales multiple for Tubi because it looks like a reasonable discounted multiple for a digital platform. Public ad-supported streaming and CTV-related companies trade across a wide range. Roku trades at a 3.1x revenue multiple, PubMatic trades at 1.6x, and Magnite trades at a 2.6x multiple. Tubi has already reached meaningful scale, so I do not think it should be valued like a weak legacy television asset. But at the same time, it is still inside FOX and not separately disclosed as a standalone business. That is why I use a multiple that sits below the cleaner public digital platform comps, rather than giving it a full peer multiple.

Other Assets

I then add the FanDuel option, which I value at about $560 million, and Other investments, which I value at about $1.25 billion. I use $1.25 billion here because the linked filing supports the line item for investments in equity securities, which is about $1,249 million.

Net Debt

Fox ended the period with about $2.017 billion of cash and $6.604 billion of borrowings, which means net debt was about $4.587 billion.

Equity Value

Putting that together—start with Cable at $27.2 billion, add Television (ex-Tubi) at $5.5 billion, then Tubi at $2.5 billion, and the FanDuel option at $0.56 billion. Also, Other investments at $1.25 billion. Then we deduct net debt ($4.59 billion). That gives a base-case equity value of roughly $32.4 billion, or about $76.2 per share.

Against the current share price of $58.6, that suggests an upside of about 30.0%.

Why The Thesis Does Not Need Asset Sales

This is an important point. I do not need a sale of business parts for the thesis to work. The stock already looks undervalued on the base SOTP alone. The main point of the SOTP is to show that the market is not giving the company full credit for Cable, Tubi, and the other assets it already owns.

That is why I do not base the investment case on management selling parts of the company. A simpler and more realistic path is enough: Cable stays strong, Tubi continues to grow, the weaker pieces of Television become less important to the story, and the company keeps returning capital over time.

Capital Returns Still Help

Capital returns still matter (although they are not the base of the thesis). FOX already has the ability to keep using ordinary free cash flow for buybacks. That should help per-share value over time even without any sale of business parts.

That matters because the upside can come from two places at the same time: First, the market can start to value Cable and Tubi more properly. Second, the share count can fall over time through normal buybacks.

I treat any extra upside from future buybacks as additional support to the story, not as something required for the base case. That is why I focus first on the $76.2 per share SOTP value. Any benefit from continued capital returns would come on top of that.

Value

Value (in $)

Current share price

58.6

SOTP value

76.2

Risks

The biggest risk here is that the market continues to value Fox as one broad media company for longer than expected. Another risk is that the weaker parts of Television continue to drag on the story and make the overall results look less attractive.

There are also the usual risks that come with media businesses - advertising cycles, sports rights costs, ratings pressure, and investor sentiment toward traditional media names. More specifically, Tubi may continue to grow and still not get a fair valuation from the market. Cable could also face more pressure if affiliate erosion becomes worse or if sports costs rise faster than expected. That said, the current share price already seems to reflect a lot of these worries.

Thesis Summary

The thesis is simple. Fox looks cheap because the market still sees one mixed media company. But when the business is looked at part by part, the value is higher. Cable is a strong and cash-generative asset. Tubi is a valuable digital platform that is not getting fair valuation. The rest of the company includes some weaker pieces, but also some monetizable assets. Also, the balance sheet and cash flow allow further meaningful buybacks. That is why the stock looks undervalued. No aggressive divestitures or buybacks are needed in order for the thesis to work. It only needs a clearer valuation and continued capital returns.

The more important point is that this is not only a historical valuation story. Cable remains strong, Tubi continues to grow, and the company does not need dramatic change to close part of the valuation gap. That is why I believe the stock can rerate even without a major corporate event.

Final Thoughts

Fox owns a strong cable franchise, a growing digital asset in Tubi, and a set of additional assets that still have value. Together, these parts appear to be worth more than the stock suggests. When we do the sum-of-the-parts analysis, we get an equity value of about $32.4 billion, or around $76.2 per share, which is above the current share price of $58.6 and implies about 30.0% upside.

The reason the opportunity exists is not because the business is weak but because the market is still valuing Fox in a broad and overly simple way. The thesis does not need a sale of business parts to work. It only needs the market to give more credit to Cable, Tubi, and the overall cash-generating power of the company. That is why I believe Fox remains a Strong Buy.
